narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 68.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which is above the typical 3.21 average, along with middle family income, female gender, Asian racial background, and plans to attend full-time—traits that align reasonably with many applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1 (far below the average 5.71) and Year 3 decile of 1 (below the average 5.53) stood out as the most influential factors, both strongly nudging the model toward predicting failure by signaling early and persistent academic struggles compared to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 25, well under the 36.70 average, which further reinforced concerns about your readiness for the rigors ahead. Your family income bracket came after that, followed by your racial background, with each also tilting the prediction toward failure in the model's eyes, though less powerfully. Your stronger undergraduate GPA had some pull but ranked lower in sway, still not enough to offset the top concerns. Gender and full-time status had the smallest roles, both contributing modestly toward the failure outlook. Overall, these factors combined to paint a picture riskier than most applicants, where stronger early grades, higher LSATs, and typical profiles often predict bar passage. The good news is several of these are changeable: retaking the LSAT for a higher score could dramatically shift future predictions, as it's a key hurdle many improve on. Focusing on building a stronger academic record now, perhaps through targeted prep or additional coursework, might boost those low decile perceptions in retellings. While background elements like income or demographics are harder to alter, emphasizing your above-average GPA and commitment to full-time study in appeals could help. Consider bar prep resources early, as students who address weak spots like yours often outperform predictions. We encourage reapplying after strengthening these areas—you have a great GPA foundation to build on.
